critique.summary: The article introduces Gea, a lightweight reactive UI framework for JavaScript that emphasizes compile-time reactivity without using a virtual DOM or additional primitives. It claims to be faster than popular frameworks like Solid, Svelte, Vue, and React, and offers a complete toolkit including state management, routing, and UI components.
mainPoints: Gea is a compile-time UI framework that converts ordinary JavaScript classes and functions into reactive components with direct DOM updates.
It does not use a virtual DOM, hooks, signals, or dependency arrays, relying solely on JavaScript features and a compiler plugin.
Gea claims superior performance compared to Solid, Svelte, Vue, and React based on js-framework-benchmark scores.
The framework includes built-in state management, routing, 35+ accessible UI components, mobile primitives, and developer tools like a VS Code extension.
Gea supports both class and function components, event delegation, fine-grained array reactivity, two-way props, computed getters, and AI integration for development assistance.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Gea's design and features align with typical descriptions of compile-time reactive frameworks and are consistent with the provided benchmark data from js-framework-benchmark, a recognized standard for UI framework performance testing. The performance claim that Gea is faster than Solid, Svelte, Vue, and React is supported by the benchmark scores cited, though independent verification from multiple sources would strengthen this claim. The description of features like no virtual DOM, proxy-based stores, and built-in routing is plausible and consistent with modern JavaScript framework trends. However, the article is promotional and does not cite independent reviews or multiple perspectives, which limits the ability to fully verify all claims.
considerations: The benchmark results should be independently verified as performance can vary based on test conditions and framework versions.
The article does not provide comparisons of developer experience or ecosystem maturity relative to established frameworks.
Potential trade-offs or limitations of compile-time reactivity and lack of virtual DOM are not discussed.
The framework's adoption, community support, and long-term maintenance are not addressed.
Security implications and compatibility with existing JavaScript tools and libraries are not mentioned.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the new JavaScript framework Gea, with commenters debating its design philosophy, reactivity model, and performance claims compared to established frameworks like React and Solid. Some appreciate its embrace of language paradigms and compile-time optimizations, while others criticize two-way binding and question its practical scalability and benchmarking accuracy.

comments.author: ludwigvan
comments.text:
React hooks always struck me as object-oriented programming reinvented through the back door of functions. We started with pure components, decided we needed state after all, and ended up with magic functions that stash and retrieve state from some hidden context — essentially re-deriving this with worse ergonomics and an implicit ordering contract. Some part of it was the functional language paradigms like immutability that were popular elsewhere at the time bolted on to JavaScript.

What I find refreshing about Gea is that it doesn't fight the language. Stores are classes. Computed values are getters. State mutation is just assignment. I've been waiting for a framework that embraces the actual paradigms of the language it's written in, rather than inventing a parallel universe of conventions. Excited to try this one.

That said, I'm genuinely curious where the edges are. Was React's complexity accidental due to its architecture or was it the price of solving genuinely hard problems (concurrent rendering, suspense boundaries, fine-grained error recovery) (which by the way most consumers of the library did not care that much about)?

Does Gea's simplicity hold up as apps get complex, or will we eventually hit patterns where the escape hatch is the complexity React already internalized?
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced and accurate critique of React's design and highlights Gea's approach, raising valid questions about complexity and scalability.

comments.author: nine_k
comments.text:
Disclaimer: I only read description, did not try to code.

What I like: the smart compiler that determines the actual dependencies, no need to declare them. Apparently the compiler is so smart as to compute the DOM diffs at compile time, which eliminates the need for virtual DOM.

What kills it for me: the two-way binding. The binding should be one-way to preserve your sanity as the project grows. Two-way bindings allow to build highly reactive Ruby Goldberg machines where anything can trigger anything else, and you won't know, because it's just a mutation of a property somewhere, indistinguishable from a non-reactive mutation. Two-way bindings are callback hell squared.

I want one-way data binding, immutability, and basically FRP. The biggest demonstration of FRP's immense real-life success is not React. It's the spreadsheet.

This may be good for small pieces of interactivity. But I likely would go for HTMX for that.
comments.analysis: The comment accurately identifies potential pitfalls of two-way binding and advocates for more maintainable patterns, reflecting common best practices in reactive programming.

comments.author: mpalmer
comments.text:
The contrast of most of the text against the background is not accessible.

I'm not overly impressed with the claim "Faster than Solid" when only figure presented on the hero chart is the geometric average of the Duration scores for each framework.

Digging into the individual metrics, Solid is well within the margin of error on essentially every metric to tie or even beat Gea.

On top of that, Solid beats Gea handily on bundle size, 1:4 uncompressed and 1:2 compressed.

So at best, Gea is a tie on speed with Solid, the bundle is bigger, and even time to first paint is a little worse.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a fact-based critique of Gea's performance claims and accessibility, supported by benchmark analysis, making it a credible and constructive observation.

comments.author: ch_sm
comments.text: hey! Great job on this, dashersw. I‘ve believed for a while now that the compile time dependency analyzer approach is the only good way for frameworks like this. Really neat choices on the API surface as well – so simple! Launching with a headless UI lib is smart. will try both in a side project soon! thanks and cheers!
comments.analysis: This positive feedback highlights key technical strengths and shows genuine interest, though it lacks critical evaluation of limitations.

comments.author: tkzed49
comments.text:
> Solid has signals and createEffect... Gea takes a different path. It introduces no new concepts at all.

proceeds to introduce Stores and Components

what makes this magically easier than Solid, or any other Proxy-based reactive store frameworks?
comments.analysis: Raises a valid point about the novelty and ease of use of Gea's abstractions compared to existing solutions, encouraging deeper scrutiny.

comments.author: slopinthebag
comments.analysis: Clarifies a technical detail about Gea's reactivity scope, correcting a common misconception, which is helpful for understanding framework limitations.

comments.author: dangoodmanUT
comments.text:
This clearly AI generated website is such a turn-off.

I get that AI can be good at making websites, and someone might not care to spend a lot of time on it, but a website that looks like a slightly modified version of a generic "make an X landing page" from gpt-5.3-codex doesn't scream "I care about what I just made".

Just go super reductionist like planetscale did and have partially rendered markdown, don't put twinkling stars in the background
comments.analysis: While the critique about design is subjective and somewhat emotional, it reflects a common user experience concern, though it adds little technical insight.

comments.author: cattown
comments.text: Two-way props! Yikes! That was a mess in the first version of Angular. I thought the consensus was that two-way props binding just opened the door to difficult to understand side-effect laden code.
comments.analysis: Provides historical context supporting the criticism of two-way binding, reinforcing concerns about maintainability.

comments.author: skyturkish
comments.text: There are many ways to write JavaScript, for example by destructuring variables, etc. How do Gea proxies work to retain reactivity then?
comments.analysis: Poses a relevant technical question about implementation details, indicating engagement but without providing an answer.

comments.author: puskuruk
comments.text: How does this thing run so close to VanillaJS without carrying any extra baggage along for the ride?
comments.analysis: Seeks clarification on framework overhead, a pertinent concern for performance-conscious developers.

comments.author: darepublic
comments.text: React is just JavaScript.  Also vanilla JavaScript is just JavaScript
comments.analysis: This tautological statement adds no meaningful insight to the discussion.

comments.author: aappleby
comments.text: You wrote and shipped this in three days, eh?
comments.analysis: Highlights the impressive speed of development but implicitly questions the depth and maturity of the project.

comments.author: afavour
comments.text:
> The Vite plugin analyzes your JSX at build time, figures out which DOM nodes depend on which state, and wires up surgical patches — invisibly.

This part interests me… if it’s able to be brought to React somehow. Too many sites are shipping entirely reactive DOMs where only a tiny minority of content actually changes.

The fact that the entire project appears to have been written in three days, however, gives me some deep doubts.
comments.analysis: Expresses cautious optimism about technical innovation while noting concerns about project maturity.

comments.author: linhns
comments.text: Not a JS dev but the site need to move away from tinted retro look if you want to stress the modernness.
comments.analysis: Aesthetic feedback from a non-technical perspective, not contributing to technical evaluation.
